sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Still wrong. I've tried many permutations. Let me reconsider the problem from scratch.
I notice that every time I fill all slots and press SPACE, the palette grays out but the level doesn't complete. This means the game ACCEPTS any permutation but only one is CORRECT.
Let me think about what makes level 2 different from level 1:
1. Level 1 has 1 frame (red) with 4 slots, 4 palette colors
2. Level 2 has 2 frames (red with 3 slots, green with 4 slots), 7 palette colors, and a top row of 7 colored squares
The top row in level 2 is key. It has the same 7 colors as the palette but in a different order.
Top row: O, p, R, b, N, Y, M
Palette: R, p, N, O, M, b, Y
What if the top row represents the "target arrangement" and the frames need to somehow encode this arrangement?
Or what if the solution involves MATCHING each top-row color to its palette position?
Let me think about the mapping:
Top row position → Palette position
O: top[1] → palette[4]
p: top[2] → palette[2]
R: top[3] → palette[1]
b: top[4] → palette[6]
N: top[5] → palette[3]
Y: top[6] → palette[7]
M: top[7] → palette[5]
What if the frames need to contain colors based on their palette position modulo something?
Or what if the red frame gets colors whose palette position is odd, and green gets even?
Palette positions: R=1, p=2, N=3, O=4, M=5, b=6, Y=7
Odd: R, N, M, Y (4 colors)
Even: p, O, b (3 colors)
But red has 3 slots and green has 4. So:
Red (3): p, O, b (even positions)
Green (4): R, N, M, Y (odd positions)
Let me try this!

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try: red frame gets even palette positions, green gets odd
# Even positions: p(2), O(4), b(6)
# Odd positions: R(1), N(3), M(5), Y(7)
# Red (3): p, O, b | Green (4): R, N, M, Y
action(['ACTION7']*20)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
